does: This paper systematically reviews 51 peer-reviewed studies to identify and categorize patterns of human-AI collaboration in disaster management across all phases—mitigation, preparedness, response, and recovery. It organizes findings into four major categories: decision support systems, task and resource coordination, trust and transparency mechanisms, and simulation-based training. The review documents how AI systems enhance situational awareness and response efficiency through approaches like cognitive-augmented intelligence and explainable AI, while identifying concrete limitations including scalability constraints, interpretability challenges, and interoperability issues that currently hinder deployment in real disaster contexts.
connects: This work synthesizes research spanning human-computer interaction, AI explainability, multi-agent systems, and crisis informatics. It builds on established lines of inquiry into decision support systems in high-stakes domains, extends theoretical frameworks around human-AI trust from critical infrastructure and autonomous systems research, and incorporates growing literature on explainable AI methods developed for safety-critical applications. The review also connects to work on distributed coordination in emergency response, virtual training environments for first responders, and broader disaster informatics research examining how technology supports emergency management across different phases.
enables: This systematic characterization points toward developing more adaptive, context-aware human-AI systems designed specifically for disaster resilience. It establishes a foundation for building trustworthy AI that operates under uncertainty in dynamic environments, supporting future work on improving system interoperability across emergency response organizations. The synthesis of transparency and trust patterns enables research into human-centered AI design that accounts for equity and accessibility in disaster scenarios. Results suggest promising directions for multi-agent coordination frameworks, real-time decision support validated in realistic disaster simulations, and investigations into how AI systems can remain reliable and interpretable when responding to unprecedented or rapidly evolving crises.
